title: Conditional Forms: If and When
learningGoal: The learner will master the '~tara' conditional form for general 'If/When' statements, and the '~to' conditional form for natural consequences.
instructions: Welcome to Month 3! We are moving into advanced fluency. This block introduces conditional statements (If X happens, then Y will happen).

lessonContent.bodyText: The '~tara' form is the most common way to say 'If' or 'When' in Japanese. To make it, take the Plain Past Tense of a verb (the 'Ta-form') and add 'ra'. (Think of the Ta-form as identical to the Te-form, just with an 'a' instead of an 'e').

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: たべる -> たべた -> たべたら
いく -> いった -> いったら
あめが ふったら、いきません。
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If it rains, I will not go. (Furu -> Futta -> Futtara)

lessonContent.bodyText: You can also use '~tara' with adjectives and nouns. Take the plain past tense and add 'ra'.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: たかい -> たかかった -> たかかったら
ひま -> ひまだ -> ひまだったら
やすかったら、かいます。
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If it is cheap, I will buy it. (Yasui -> Yasukattara)

lessonContent.bodyText: To say 'If it doesn't happen', take the Nai-form, change 'nai' to 'nakatta', and add 'ra'.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: いかない -> いかなかったら
おいしくなかったら、たべません。
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If it is not delicious, I will not eat it.

lessonContent.bodyText: When an action naturally or mechanically causes a result, use the Dictionary Form + 'to' (instead of ~tara). This is used heavily for giving directions or explaining how machines work.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: この ボタンを おすと、ドアが あきます。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Kono botan wo osu to, doa ga akimasu.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If/When you push this button, the door opens. (Natural consequence)

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: まっすぐ いくと、えきが あります。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Massugu iku to, eki ga arimasu.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If you go straight, the station is there. (Directional consequence)

learningObjective: The learner will be able to form complex conditional sentences using '~tara' for hypothetical 'if' statements and '~to' for inevitable consequences.

examples.targetText: じかんが あったら、えいがを みます。
examples.transliteration: Jikan ga attara, eiga wo mimasu.
examples.baseTranslation: If I have time, I will watch a movie.
examples.explanation: Here, 'attara' comes from 'aru' (to exist/to have). It is a hypothetical condition.

examples.targetText: はるに なると、さくらが さきます。
examples.transliteration: Haru ni naru to, sakura ga sakimasu.
examples.baseTranslation: When it becomes spring, cherry blossoms bloom.
examples.explanation: Here, 'to' is used because spring naturally and inevitably causes cherry blossoms to bloom.

importantRules.ruleText: Do NOT use '~to' for requests, permissions, or invitations.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: 'Jikan ga aru to, kite kudasai' is WRONG. You must use tara: 'Jikan ga attara, kite kudasai' (If you have time, please come).

importantRules.ruleText: The Ta-form conjugates exactly like the Te-form.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Tabete -> Tabeta. Nonde -> Nonda. Itte -> Itta.

cultureContext.contextText: Japanese automated machines (vending machines, train ticket gates) usually have instructions written using the '~to' conditional. For example, 'Suica wo tatchi suru to, doa ga akimasu' (If you touch your Suica card, the door opens).
dosList: Use '~to' when giving driving or walking directions.
Use '~tara' when asking for hypothetical advice (e.g., Doshitara ii desu ka - What should I do?).

summary: Use '~tara' (Past form + ra) for general 'If/When' statements. Use Dictionary form + 'to' for natural, automatic consequences like machines or geographic directions.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What is the '~tara' form of Nomimasu (to drink)?
How do you say 'If you push the button' (Button = botan, Push = osu)?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: ボタンを おすと (Botan wo osu to)

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What is the '~tara' form of Takai (expensive)?

adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Introduction to conditional logic in Japanese.
importantFacts: ~to cannot be followed by an expression of human volition (e.g. let's go, please go).
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learner must memorize the past tense conjugations to form the '~tara' conditional effectively.

feedbackRules.onPass: Great job! You have unlocked a major milestone in Japanese fluency.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review the Te-form rules from Month 2. The Ta-form is identical to the Te-form, just replacing the 'e' sound with an 'a' sound.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good start. Remember the difference between '~tara' (hypothetical) and '~to' (automatic result).

audioAssets.audioTranscript: たべたら。いったら。あめが ふったら、いきません。たかかったら。ひまだったら。やすかったら、かいます。いかなかったら。おいしくなかったら、たべません。この ボタンを おすと、ドアが あきます。まっすぐ いくと、えきが あります。

commonMistakes: Using '~to' with a request: 'Ame ga furu to, kasa wo motte kudasai' (Incorrect). Must be 'Ame ga futtara, kasa wo motte kudasai'.
